cards.back: Systems Development Life Cycle — a structured process for planning, creating, testing, and delivering software or information systems in defined stages.

cards.front: What are the seven phases of the SDLC?
cards.back:
1. Scoping & Feasibility
2. Analysis
3. Design
4. Development
5. Testing
6. Deployment
7. Maintenance
An assessment before a project begins that considers technical, economic, legal, and operational feasibility — can the project be done, can it be afforded, is it legal, and will it be used?

cards.front: What is the analysis phase of the SDLC?
cards.back: Gathering detailed requirements from stakeholders using interviews, questionnaires, and observation. The output is a requirements specification documenting what the system must do.
Testing carried out by end users to verify the system meets their real-world needs. Done near the end of development, before full deployment.

cards.front: What are the three types of test data?
cards.back:
1. Normal — valid data the system should accept
2. Boundary — data at the edge of acceptable ranges (min/max)
3. Erroneous — invalid data the system should reject

cards.front: What is the waterfall model's biggest weakness?
cards.back: It is very inflexible — changing requirements after the project has moved past the analysis phase is expensive and disruptive. Testing also comes very late in the process.

cards.front: What is a sprint in agile development?
cards.back: A short, time-boxed iteration (typically 1–4 weeks) at the end of which a working, tested increment of software is delivered to the customer for review.

cards.front: What makes the spiral model different from other SDLC models?
cards.back: Each iteration explicitly includes a risk analysis phase. It combines the structure of waterfall with the flexibility of iterative development. Best for large, high-risk projects.

cards.front: What is parallel running as a deployment strategy?
cards.back: Running the old and new systems simultaneously for a period until confidence in the new system is established. Reduces risk — users can fall back to the old system if needed.

cards.front: What is corrective, adaptive, and perfective maintenance?
cards.back:
Corrective: fixing bugs found after release.
Adaptive: updating the system for changed environments (new OS, APIs).
Perfective: improving performance or adding new features based on user feedback.

cards.front: What is intellectual property (IP) in software development?
cards.back: Legal rights protecting creative work. In software: copyright protects code from being copied without permission; licences define permitted use. Developers must not use unlicensed code/assets.
Testing done internally by the development team (or a small group within the organisation) before the software is released to any external users.
Testing done by a limited group of real external users before the full public release. Their feedback is used to find remaining bugs and usability issues.

cards.front: Name three ethical considerations in systems development.
cards.back:
1. Privacy — is only necessary data collected and secured?
2. Accessibility — can people with disabilities use the system?
3. Algorithmic bias — could AI components discriminate unfairly?
(Also: job displacement, environmental impact, social responsibility)
